evals.query: Add Micrometer metrics to my Kora service and expose them for Prometheus scraping.
expected_behavior: Adds the ru.tinkoff.kora:micrometer-module dependency and the Kora annotation processor (annotation-processors for Java or symbol-processors for Kotlin)
Extends MetricsModule on the @KoraApp interface alongside the HTTP server module
Configures httpServer.privateApiHttpMetricsPath = "/metrics" on the private port (privateApiHttpPort), not the public port
Does not add a separate io.micrometer:micrometer-registry-prometheus dependency, since micrometer-module already registers PrometheusMeterRegistry
Does not use external actuator frameworks or field injection; relies on constructor injection and the @KoraApp graph

evals.query: Create a service that records how long user creation takes and counts created users by email provider (gmail.com, ...).
expected_behavior: Injects MeterRegistry through the constructor of a @Component
Registers a Timer once in the constructor with serviceLevelObjectives(...) buckets
Caches Counter instances per email provider with ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent to keep the email.provider tag bounded
Falls back to a value like "unknown" for emails without a domain
Does not use field injection or @Autowired

evals.query: Attach common tags (service, environment, version) to every metric in my Kora app.
expected_behavior: Adds a PrometheusMeterRegistryInitializer as a default method of a @Module
Calls registry.config().commonTags(...) inside the initializer
Returns the registry from the initializer because it is a Function<PrometheusMeterRegistry, PrometheusMeterRegistry>
Does not invent a @Default annotation or any external MeterRegistry customizer type; uses only PrometheusMeterRegistryInitializer

evals.query: Review this metrics code that adds userId and requestId as tags on a counter and tell me what is wrong.
expected_behavior: Identifies userId and requestId as unbounded high-cardinality tags that cause unbounded time series growth and a memory leak
Recommends bounded tag values (such as method, http.response.status_code, http.route template, provider, status)
Suggests aggregating to a bounded dimension or dropping the tag, optionally caching meters per bounded value with computeIfAbsent
Does not recommend an external tracing/actuator framework; keeps the fix within Kora's MeterRegistry

evals.query: Configure latency histogram buckets so I can compute p95 and p99 for my API timer.
expected_behavior: Uses Timer.builder(...).serviceLevelObjectives(Duration.ofMillis(...), ...) on the custom meter
Mentions the per-module config equivalent telemetry.metrics.slo as a millisecond array for built-in module metrics
Explains that buckets let Prometheus estimate percentiles via histogram_quantile, and notes publishPercentiles adds overhead
Does not put the metrics endpoint on the public port

evals.query: How do I switch my Kora metrics to the newer OpenTelemetry naming convention?
expected_behavior: Sets metrics.opentelemetrySpec = "V123" in the configuration (described by MetricsConfig)
Explains V120 is the original default and V123 is available since Kora 1.1.0
Notes the setting changes metric/tag naming only, not the export transport
Does not introduce configuration keys from other frameworks; uses only the Kora metrics block

evals.query: Can I push Kora metrics directly to Datadog or an OTLP endpoint from the app?
expected_behavior: Explains that Kora's micrometer-module is Prometheus pull-only and serves /metrics on the private HTTP port
States there is no Kora config to swap the registry to a push exporter from the application
Recommends scraping /metrics with an agent (Prometheus, OpenTelemetry Collector, vmagent) and forwarding from there
Distinguishes metric export from tracing export handled by the separate tracing modules
Does not fabricate micrometer.statsd or micrometer.otlp Kora config keys

evals.query: Which metric and labels does Kora emit for HTTP server request latency, and how do I query the error rate in Prometheus?
expected_behavior: Names http.server.request.duration (Prometheus http_server_request_duration_milliseconds) as a DistributionSummary
Lists the real OpenTelemetry tags http.request.method, http.response.status_code, http.route
Writes a Prometheus error-rate query using http_response_status_code=~"5.." on the _count series
Does not invent metric names like http.server.requests or labels like status/route
